speaker: interviewer
text: August the 1st, 1996. The survivor, Harry Guterman. Interviewer, Michelle Marrs. Tulsa, Oklahoma, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Michelle Marrs. Today's date is August 1st, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Harry Guterman. The interview is being conducted in Tulsa, Oklahoma, United States of America. Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry Guterman, G-U-T-E-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Harry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry is H-A-R-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was Herszel, Hersz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-R-S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guterman, G-U-T-E-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been known by any other names or nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But sometimes a different language, some people would call-- they couldn't-- they couldn't pronounce the name Hersz or they couldn't pronounce so well Harry, so they would call me maybe a different name, but just nothing official.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born June 1925, the 13th of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My current age is 71 and 2 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me the city and country of your birth, and spell the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was born in Konstantynow, near Lodz in Poland. And to spell Konstantynow, it's K-O-N-S-T-A-N-T-Y-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Chaim, C-H-A-I-M, Guterman. And he-- he was born around 1897, or '98, at that time. He-- he worked together with my grandfather in his business, also worked for himself in his business, in the textile industry. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after-- in 1930s, when Hitler came to power in Germany, in Poland the anti-Semitism became very active and dangerous for a lot of people. And everybody was looking a way how to get out of Poland. And the first thing they were thinking about at the time was Palestine. The Zionistic movement started out at that time, to go to Palestine and maybe build a country there, and a new home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So being that he was the oldest child, the oldest son in the family, it was pretty much decided that he should be the one to go to Palestine and to see how it is going to be there. And so when he arrived there, he liked it and he never wanted to come back to Poland. And the whole thing was that we were working on-- on the premises and the idea to pack up in Poland and go to Palestine at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, the war broke out in 1939. And there was not enough time to do all these things. And so he got sort of stuck in Palestine and we were in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come back to that. For now, please tell me about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Hinda Guterman. She was born as Rawska, R-A-W-S-K-A, Rawska. And she was-- she was not-- she didn't have a job. She didn't have-- she was a housewife. And that's about all I can tell you, being that she is-- she was a good mother and she was running the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one sister, Luba, L-U-B-A. And she was about a year and a half older than I, about maybe two years older than I. And we-- we also lived in Konstantynow. And, of course, when my father left for Palestine, we lived-- we lived in Konstantynow. And then we moved up to the house where I was born, to my first house that I-- I was born. And that house was owned by my grandfather and grandmother, Gutermans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your early family life as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, listen, I was 14 years old when the war broke out, 14 and 1/2 years old, not even. And I went to school. I had seven years of normal school, nothing exciting. It was a small town. Konstantynow is near Lodz. It was like 10 kilometers, which is 7 or 8 miles away from the city of Lodz, which was the large city in Poland. And he-- in that little city, there were only maybe about 8,000 or 10,000 people living. And so there was really not much I can tell you, other than having some friends that I went to school with and-- up to 14 years old, because after the war broke out, we were not able to go anyplace.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other relatives that lived close by to your grandparents, aunts, uncles, cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had my grandparents, the Gutermans, Abraham Guterman and Golda was the-- my grandmother. And I had some uncles that lived also in the same town, in the same. area. Other than that, of course we had-- we-- we were a very large family. I cannot tell you today all the people that were related to me. I just couldn't. But I knew quite a few, you know. But I understand after the war talking to so many other people that there were at least 100 or 120 people that were closely related. You must understand that in Poland, we just didn't have one child. We had children. There were some-- some households that had 10, 11, 12 kids. So if I had an uncle and-- you know, they would have that many children, some of them, that they would have maybe 8 or 10 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish and Yiddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That were the two languages that were mainly used in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you when you started to attend school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- a regular, normal school, where you start out with class 1, class 2, you know, until you are finished with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] normal school, the first seven years. Most Polish children only went to that school for two or three or four years, and then they just quit. And so I went seven years, in hope-- hoping that I would go on to a-- to a high school and maybe on and on and on, to a college or-- I didn't have that chance to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you hope to be when you grew up at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was really-- sort of thought that maybe to be an engineer would be a good job for a man. And I was kind of dreaming about it. But that didn't even get any place, because I was only 14 years old to be able to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to even make a decision where I want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you enjoy most about school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we enjoyed, of course, the-- the fact that we learned more. Poland is not the most progressive country in the world. It wasn't-- let's say Poland is next to Germany, and Germany being a very sophisticated country, was quite a bit of education. And-- and Poland didn't have that. In Poland, if you went to a high school, if you finished a high school, you were a intellectual. You were a very well educated person. So our desires and dreams were not as-- as large or as far as let's say they would be for a boy of-- in Germany or France or other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like least about attending school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the fact that I had to go to school. [LAUGHS] I did not particularly like school, but it was OK. I went to more than just that one school. Being that I wanted to learn the language, the Hebrew language, I went to a Hebrew school. And then I went to a private Hebrew school. There were two type schools, one that you were able to go-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where all other children from various-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] various ages would go. I went also to one that was private and strictly learning the language. Then I had a private teacher that came twice a-- a week to teach me strictly the Hebrew language. And at one point, when the war started in 1939, I was pretty good in Hebrew. I could sit down and write a Hebrew letter at that time. I was almost as good as in the Polish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family's religious practices.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my grandparents, of course, were religious. Their children, lets say like my father, was not very religious. The reason he went to Palestine was not because he-- he did it for religious reasons. He did it because he wanted to see a-- a future. He saw a future. He saw a different life and-- and more freedom than what we could see that's going to happen in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend the synagogue regularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to synagogue maybe once every three-- two weeks, three weeks just to carry the tallit for my grandfather. That I can remember. And I would go there and stay maybe an hour. And then I would skip-- skip out of there and run home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you observe Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you practice kosher dietary laws at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much that you would say it was a real religious dietary that we would be very, very strict about it. My mother was not so strict about it, and my father certainly not so. But we had to do it, because we had to-- our grandparents would always come to eat. And they wanted to make sure that we have sort of a kosher house, so we had half and half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bar mitzvahs in Poland were different than here. Here, you make a big party and all this, and you invite guests. Over there, it was just-- it's called a Kiddush. It was on the Saturday, after the-- the prayers. They had a drink of vodka or whatever, and a piece of cake, and that was it. This was what they called the bar mitzvah at that time. I'm sure that maybe some people made a little bigger party than I had. But this is all I can say happened on my bar mitzvah day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: In general, how would you describe the Jewish community life in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In Poland, in the city where I lived, there were something like 8,000 people, of which there were about 1,000 or 1,200 Jewish people. It was a very large Jewish population, because there was a lot of textile going on in that area. And in the textile business, there were a lot of Jewish people involved. And I think because of that maybe, there was a larger Jewish population than normally in some other cities. But in Poland, there were about 3 and 1/2 million Jews, large population of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. In school. I was on the-- we only had about three or four Jewish children in my class. And you know, when class was over, school was over, I had to make sure to get home. And you know-- and a lot of the kids there, they-- they were waiting for that to beat me up. Being that I was strong and husky and was very fast and running, I was able to negotiate out a pretty good deal for myself. I was really never really beaten up. But-- but I did beat up a few others who wanted to beat me up. So this is from that point of view, as a child. Of course, the anti-Semitism was more-- was more important for the-- for the grown-ups. They could do something about it. I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever hear your parents talking about anti-Semitism acts when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we talked not only with my parents, but also with the people that lived in that city. You must understand there were at least about 10% or 15% of the population of Konstantynow were Jewish. So you more or less would run into them every day on a daily basis, and there would be stories about, you know, what happened, somebody was beat up, or somebody broke the glass, the-- the windows, or things like this. That would be very, very common in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it around this time that your father decided to leave for Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it wasn't just my father. It was thousands and thousands of people that decided that time has come that we will have to do something to secure our livelihood, and either get away-- a lot of people had friends in America and they got affidavits to come to America and they emigrated out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't have that pleasure to go to America, but we could have gone to Palestine at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did he intend to send for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, we were-- he was going there to see if-- if things are OK. And so they were. They were so good that he didn't even want to consider going back to Poland. The whole thing was right away we started working on papers that we can go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was about what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1938, '39, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the German-- hearing about the German invasion of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did-- how did you hear about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here again, we had to run away from Konstantynow on onto Lodz, because we had more relatives there. In Lodz, we had relatives. We had people to stay with. And we had-- we just felt more secure there. And we didn't think that the Germans will-- will go so fast. They did. Eight days later, Lodz was-- was gone. It was the Germans had Lodz. And Lodz is pretty much in the middle of Poland-- of Poland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time you saw a Nazi soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was the army, when they came in. That's the time we saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they march through the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: They marched, yeah. They marched in with all their ammunition and-- and guns and horses and cars and trucks and whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you out watching them, or in a window, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We-- we were-- we were amazed. You know, the propaganda in Poland, they thought, well, Germany, three, four days, the Polacks can take Berlin. Well, it was just the other way around. I mean, in a few days, 10 days, the-- the German army was outside of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had moved to Lodz. Describe the rest of that fall and the winter. What was your life like during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went back to Konstantynow after the war was over, the Germans came in. And the problem was, in these little towns, you were able to maybe manage better as far as food is concerned, because we had so many good friends-- we thought they are good friends, the Polacks-- and we thought that maybe, you know, we would be able to get a bread and sell it in Lodz. And maybe we will have half of the bread. Our-- our financial situation was not very good at the time, because my father was not there and my mother had to take care of two kids. So me, as a-- as a boy at the time of 14 and 1/2 years old, I tried to do the things that a husband would do, as far as buying and selling something and, you know, trying to earn some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you also attending school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. School was over with the-- the day the war started. That was it. It was finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: For Polish-- for Polish Gentile children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all over. Not all over. But in some cases, it was chaos. Fathers would run away. Mothers would run away. Kids would run away. You know, it was-- nobody realized that there is such a blitzkrieg, that the Germans are so strong and so fast that they could take Poland in-- practically in a week or 10 days. Nobody realized that. Then before we knew, the Russians got together with the-- with the Germans and they came from the east into Poland. So as I say, 10 days later, it was only Warsaw that they were fighting for.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the war in Poland was something very hard to understand today how fast it was-- it was at all going there. The Germans could just wipe Poland out, and they did. They could have killed every Polack in 10 days, for that matter. Tell me about the spring of 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Spring 1940, I was already in the ghetto. That is-- you know, as the Germans saw that Lodz had a tremendous Jewish population, more than the 10% percentage, I don't know maybe 30% of-- of Jews lived-- 30% of the population in Lodz were Jewish. They were in the textile business and it was a very industrious city. And so the Germans saw that this is not going to work, being all integrated and mixed, so they started to separate and build a ghetto around the area where the Jewish population was living in Lodz. And that's where the ghettos started. So at that time, they were very tough, the Germans. They came into one building and there could have been maybe 80 or 100 people living in that building, apartments. And they would give 15 minutes or 20 minutes and say, you, get out, raus. And we had to go someplace, so people were running to the ghetto. They said, here is where you're going to have to find a place to live. And so this is-- this was how it started out right away. It was people who had something on them-- if they were smart, they either tried to move into the area where the ghetto was, or they would just run to Warsaw, run away, run east, because they thought that were the Russians are, it's going to be better.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family come to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's the way we came. We had to-- we had to go first to Lodz, because they said, if you stay in Konstantynow, you're going to get killed. So it was only eight-- eight miles or so, so you-- we went in the trolley car. And we took our little things, whatever we had. We-- we went to Lodz. And we-- we were living with some relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was it that told you that you had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The Germans came in and they said, [GERMAN], you know. By Monday morning, you are not here in this town anymore, period. So we had to move to Lodz. And as we were in Lodz, it was, of course, right away the situation with the ghetto that there's a ghetto being built. And so everyone was running into the area where the-- where the ghetto is going to be. So this is how the ghetto was built. You know, more and more people came in and then they-- there was not enough room. In some apartments where there was maybe you could have only three or four people living, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they would put in eight, eight people, double the amount.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your family's living situation in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, we-- at that time, we had to live-- we lived I think it was three or four families in one large room, maybe as as large as this room, maybe a little bigger. And we just divided it up with a sheet. And each one had their own little facilities, where they had their bed to sleep on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So sometimes you had-- in one bed, you had two-- two-- two children, the husband, and the wife, or there were six or seven children in there. But some people slept during the day. Some people slept during the night. And then, of course, there were some that were able to-- with enough money, to buy themself into an apartment that would be a little larger, so they were able to live a little more comfortable in the ghetto. And you were there with your mother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other--

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of my grandmothers. My mother's mother was with us in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you first entered the ghetto and looked around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell you, all these things happened so fast and-- and you are so emotionally involved that you don't even think, what do I think about the ghetto? No matter better what I thought about the ghetto, it didn't-- it didn't matter to anyone. You just-- everyone was in there and everyone had to think about how you get a piece of bread to eat and how you're gonna get a job and how you're gonna exist. So it wasn't anything that I'm going to go in there and-- and evaluate whether I should or should not stay. I couldn't get out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop the-- the tape now.
